mechanism: The study compares neural and perceptual responses to infer whether motion depicted in static images activates the same direction-selective processing mechanisms as real physical motion. Participants view a series of static images with motion cues and are measured for adaptation effects (motion aftereffect); subsequently they are tested with real-motion probes to assess the transfer of adaptation from implied to real motion. The analysis examines whether the direction-selectivity signal is shared between the two stimulus conditions.
domain: visual perception, motion processing neuroscience

mechanism: Participants in each group (preschool children and adults) are presented with a visual search task: a single target image is embedded in an array of eight distractor images. Reaction time and accuracy are measured for detection of each target type. The comparison of mean reaction times across target categories identifies which stimulus types are detected more rapidly. A between-subjects analysis tests whether the attentional bias pattern is consistent across both age groups.
domain: visual attention, threat detection, child development

mechanism: Participants are randomly assigned to a spatial priming condition: plotting points on a Cartesian plane to induce either closeness or distance processing. After the priming manipulation, each participant is assessed on dependent measures (emotional response to media, caloric estimation, emotional attachment ratings). The analysis compares means between conditions and tests whether the spatial prime significantly shifts the outcome variables.
domain: cognitive psychology, construal theory, embodied cognition

mechanism: Researchers correlate behavioral and demographic variables (predictors) with three types of country-level conscientiousness measures: self-reports, peer reports, and perceptions of national character. For each measure type, correlations are computed between the predictors and the national-level outcome. The analysis tests whether the strength and pattern of correlations differ across measurement types, diagnosing whether reference-group effects contaminate self-report and peer-report measures.
domain: personality psychology, cross-cultural psychology, psychometrics

mechanism: Participants are assigned to either a confrontational or nonconfrontational task context. Prior to the task, they rate their preference for activities that would increase anger versus excitement. Subsequently, they perform the assigned task and their performance is measured. The analysis examines whether anger preference is moderated by task type, and whether anger improves task performance more in confrontational than nonconfrontational contexts.
domain: emotion regulation, behavioral decision-making psychology

mechanism: Participants are asked to indicate what kinds of personal predictions (accurate, optimistic, or pessimistic) they and others ought to make. Responses are coded and analyzed to identify whether people endorse optimistically biased predictions as normatively appropriate. The analysis compares the frequency and distribution of endorsement across prediction types and examines correlations with individual difference variables.
domain: judgment and decision-making, rationality, belief bias psychology

mechanism: Participants are presented with fair and unfair offers while functional neuroimaging records brain activation. Self-reported happiness ratings are collected in parallel. The analysis separates the effects of fairness and monetary payoff by design, comparing brain activation and happiness between fair and unfair offers of equal monetary value. Association between behavioral choice and regional activation (ventrolateral prefrontal cortex, anterior insula) is tested.
domain: neuroeconomics, fairness, emotion

mechanism: Participants view nonverbal cues and rate the likelihood that the cues indicate sexual interest. The ratings are analyzed using signal detection theory, which decompose observed accuracy into two independent components: perceptual sensitivity (d-prime) and decisional bias (criterion). The analysis tests whether gender differences in misperception stem from differences in sensitivity or bias, or both.
domain: social perception, nonverbal communication, gender differences

mechanism: Participants are measured on personality dominance and tested in spatial attention paradigms (reaction time to probes appearing along vertical or horizontal dimensions). For each participant, reaction time to horizontal probes is used as a covariate to control for general response speed. The analysis tests whether dominance predicts faster response times to vertical probes after adjusting for horizontal probe response times.
domain: personality psychology, cognitive metaphor, spatial attention

mechanism: Participants report whether they own each of 52 material items and the extent to which they want each item. Two predictor variables are constructed: (1) the sum of desire for items owned (wanting what you have), and (2) the sum of ownership of desired items (having what you want). Both predictors and mediators (gratitude, maximization) are regressed onto happiness outcomes. Mediation analysis decomposes the indirect effects through each predictor variable.
domain: well-being, consumer behavior, decision-making psychology

mechanism: Participants are tested in attention tasks where they must identify locations of individual objects or compute the center of mass across multiple objects under attended and unattended conditions. Accuracy for individual positions and centroid position are measured separately. The analysis compares how much attention withdrawal degrads each type of measurement, testing whether ensemble (summary) statistics are preserved outside the attentional focus more than local features.
domain: visual attention, perceptual processing, cognitive neuroscience
structure: other: spatial task with statistical summary

mechanism: Participants are assigned to groups and given monetary endowments. Each participant allocates contributions to one of two pools: one benefiting the in-group at personal cost, or one benefiting the in-group while harming the out-group. Contribution amounts to each pool are recorded. The analysis compares the distribution of contributions across pools and the effect of intragroup communication on allocation patterns, distinguishing in-group love from out-group hate as motivational drivers.
domain: intergroup relations, cooperation, experimental game theory

mechanism: Participants make individual numerical estimates (e.g., guessing the weight of an object). The average of these estimates is computed, and the error of the crowd average is compared to the average error of individual estimates. The analysis tests whether averaging across independent individual estimates reduces aggregate error and examines conditions under which this wisdom-of-crowds effect holds or fails.
domain: collective intelligence, decision aggregation, judgment averaging

mechanism: Participants view negative scenes and are tested for memory at three time points: 30 minutes, 12 daytime hours awake, and 12 nighttime hours with sleep. Memory is measured for emotional objects and their backgrounds separately. The analysis tracks how each component decays or is preserved across time intervals, comparing forgetting rates between the wake and sleep conditions and testing for differential consolidation of object versus background information.
domain: memory consolidation, emotion, sleep physiology

mechanism: Participants provide monetary valuations for identical events described as occurring in the future or the equivalent past. The mean valuations are compared between future and past events both within and between individuals. Affect ratings for contemplating future versus past events are also collected. Mediation analysis tests whether the asymmetry in valuation is mediated by differential affect.
domain: intertemporal choice, temporal discounting, decision-making psychology

mechanism: Participants are assigned to a control condition or a cleanliness manipulation (either cognitive activation or physical cleansing). Subsequently, they rate the moral wrongness of actions described in moral scenarios. The analysis compares mean moral judgment severity across conditions, testing whether the salience of purity reduces the severity of moral judgments.
domain: moral cognition, embodied ethics, emotional contagion

mechanism: Participants are randomly assigned to hold either a heavy or light clipboard while making judgments of importance. Across four studies, different dependent measures are collected: monetary value judgments, importance ratings of procedures, consistency between related judgments, and polarization of argument ratings. The analysis compares means across weight conditions and tests whether weight influences the depth and elaboration of cognitive processing.
domain: embodied cognition, decision-making, weight as metaphor

mechanism: Participants (right-handed and left-handed) provide perceptual estimates of their own arm and hand length. These estimates are compared to objective measurements. The analysis tests whether perception asymmetries (right-arm perceived as longer than left-arm) correlate with handedness and with known asymmetries in cortical body maps derived from neuroscientific literature.
domain: body perception, somatosensory neuroscience, motor cortex mapping

mechanism: Web-based participants respond to a series of moral dilemmas each presenting a choice between action and inaction, with outcomes involving lives saved and lost. Moral judgments (permissibility) are coded as present or absent. The response pattern is tested for consistency with the principle of the double effect. Separately, participants are asked to provide justifications; these are coded and compared to the predictions of the moral judgment pattern, revealing a dissociation between judgment and conscious justification.
domain: moral cognition, normative ethics, philosophy of mind

mechanism: Participants are presented with vignettes describing actions and their side-effects, manipulating the valence of the side-effect (good versus bad). Participants judge whether the actor knew the action would bring about the side-effect. The analysis compares knowledge attributions across valence conditions, testing whether the judgment of intentionality or knowledge is moderated by the desirability of the outcome.
domain: epistemology, moral cognition, action theory
